title: Research and development resilience of foreign MNE subsidiaries during the Great Recession
authors: Omid Aliasghar; Pedro Mendi; C. Annique Un; Kazuhiro Asakawa
affiliations: Management and International Business Department University of Auckland Auckland New Zealand; Department of Business Universidad de Navarra Pamplona Navarra Spain; International Business and Strategy D'Amore‐McKim School of Business Boston Massachusetts USA; Graduate School of Business Administration Keio University Yokohama Japan
abstract: Abstract Research Summary This study examines the research and development (R&D) resilience of subsidiaries of foreign multinational enterprises (MNEs) during the Great Recession, defined as the ability to sustain R&D investment amid economic disruption. Building on studies in global strategy and international business, we argue that R&D resilience is shaped by corporate‐based advantages rooted in MNE affiliation and parent‐level resource access, as well as by subsidiary‐based capabilities developed through external cooperation and exploratory activity in response to liabilities of foreignness. Analysis of a sample of subsidiaries from 2005 to 2013 indicates that corporate‐based advantages and subsidiary‐based capabilities may function as alternatives in sustaining R&D investment during periods of disruption. These findings enhance our understanding of subsidiary resilience and highlight the contingent nature of multinationality advantages during economic disruption. Managerial Summary This study investigates the factors that enable certain foreign multinational enterprise (MNE) subsidiaries to sustain research and development (R&D) investment during periods of economic disruption. The findings indicate that resilience is determined by two primary sources of advantage: corporate‐based advantages derived from multinational affiliation, which provide access to parent‐level resources that buffer innovation from local market shocks, and subsidiary‐based capabilities developed through external cooperation and exploratory activities, which generate locally embedded knowledge stocks essential for protecting R&D under adverse conditions. MNE leaders are advised to prioritize support for subsidiaries that are strategically important but face constraints in accessing these advantages. For subsidiary managers, building R&D resilience requires proactive investment in cooperative partnerships, exploratory initiatives, and enhancing visibility within the intra‐firm network prior to the disruption.

title: When governments act as strategists: Geopolitical turmoil and global strategy theory
affiliations: HEC Montréal Montréal Canada; University of Leeds Leeds UK; Copenhagen Business School Copenhagen Denmark; University of Innsbruck Innsbruck Austria; WU Vienna Vienna Austria
abstract: Abstract Research Summary The resurgence of geopolitical rivalry is challenging core assumptions of global strategy theory. Long‐standing views that economic interdependence is inherently beneficial, governments act as rule setters, multinational enterprises operate autonomously, and market and political strategy are separate no longer capture today's international business environment. We argue that, in an era of politicized interdependence, governments increasingly act as system‐level strategists, using economic statecraft to shape multinational enterprises and global value chains in pursuit of economic security. Consequently, economic interdependence has become a source of both prosperity and strategic vulnerability, firm autonomy is constrained by government priorities, and competitive advantage depends on integrating market and political strategy. We develop a framework distinguishing strategic sectors characterized by state co‐strategizing from non‐strategic sectors where market logics continue to dominate. Managerial Summary This article argues that recent geopolitical tensions are reshaping how multinational companies operate globally. Traditionally, global strategy theories assumed that governments mainly created stable rules while firms independently pursued efficiency and growth. The paper shows that this assumption no longer fully holds. Especially in strategic sectors, governments use public policy actions to reshape international business with the aim of strengthening their country's economic security. For managers in strategic sectors, this means that geopolitical considerations cannot merely be treated as an external risk managed by compliance or government affairs teams. They must more deeply integrate their political strategies with their competitive strategy.

title: Book review: Michelle Jackson, The Division of Rationalized Labor MichelleJackson, The Division of Rationalized Labor, Harvard University Press: Cambridge, MA, 2025; 352 pp.: ISBN: 9780674296220

Book review: Frederick Neuhouser, Diagnosing Social Pathology: Rousseau, Hegel, Marx, and Durkheim FrederickNeuhouser, Diagnosing Social Pathology: Rousseau, Hegel, Marx, and Durkheim, Cambridge University Press: Cambridge, 2023; 386 pp.: ISBN: 9781009235020
Breaking Out of Low‐Effort Traps: Bureaucratic Leadership by Persuasion
affiliations: Department of Government London School of Economics and Political Science London UK
abstract: ABSTRACT This paper tests if bureaucratic leadership impacts the quality of service provision of a difficult‐to‐monitor task: learning in public schools. Using the empirical case of rural India, it exploits an administrative setup with two types of bureaucrats for the same spatial unit, the district, that either have more authority or more ability to engage in time‐intensive persuasion. Utilizing blocked randomization inference and bias‐corrected variance decomposition on bureaucratic postings linked to independent learning data from household surveys across 10 years, it shows that only those bureaucrats with less authority but more ability to engage in persuasion impact learning. Drawing on novel interview data, it illustrates how bureaucratic leaders can increase effort levels of subordinates through persuasion to overcome collective action problems rather than relying on orders and monitoring as principal‐agent frameworks would suggest. The findings illustrate that for difficult‐to‐monitor tasks managerial intensity and persistence trump formal authority.

title: Attention Is Attention: Preserving Ontological Integrity in Organizational Research
abstract: Abstract Inspired by the Attention‐Based View of the firm, organizational research has illuminated how organizations learn, adapt, and perform, yet recent theorizing risks diluting attention's explanatory force by compromising its ontological integrity. In this Counterpoint , I argue that attention must be preserved as a scarce, selective, and situated mechanism linking cognition, structure, and behaviour. I clarify the ontological attributes that follow from the definition of attention and show how efforts to re‐ontologize attention as ‘crescive’ risk collapsing causal explanation into phenomenological description. I then argue that many persistent gaps in the literature are misdiagnosed as limitations of the Attention‐Based View when they in fact stem from the field's failure to fully exploit the ontology inherent in attention's definition. I develop this claim through two such gaps, limited normative theorizing and insufficient theorization of situations, and advance three research agendas: developing attention patterns that manage scarcity, revealing the costs of selective attention, and designing information environments that condition returns on attention. By preserving rather than dissolving its ontological core, this Counterpoint shows that attention's enduring power lies not in expanding its boundaries but in fully exploiting the scarcity, selectivity, and situatedness it already affords.

title: Ignited or depressed? Entrepreneurs' status inconsistency and philanthropic giving
authors: Jianhua Ge; Eric Yanfei Zhao; Wei Lei
affiliations: School of Business Renmin University of China Beijing China; Saïd Business School, St Hugh’s College, New College University of Oxford Oxford UK; School of Business East China University of Science and Technology Shanghai China
abstract: Abstract Research Summary Research has long emphasized entrepreneurs' status as a driver of philanthropy. Extending this literature, we examine how status inconsistency—misalignment among perceived economic, social, and political status—is associated with entrepreneurs' philanthropic contributions. We develop two theoretically grounded but competing interpretations: compensation , which suggests that entrepreneurs may increase giving to offset perceived status imbalance, and resentment , which suggests that perceived imbalance may dampen prosocial engagement. Using a national survey of Chinese private entrepreneurs supplemented with qualitative interviews, we find that while higher perceived status in individual dimensions is positively associated with philanthropy, inconsistency across these status dimensions is, on average, negatively associated with it. Moreover, entrepreneurs from farmer backgrounds, compared with those from upper‐class origins, exhibit patterns consistent with stronger compensatory giving. These findings underscore the nuanced implications of multidimensional perceived status for entrepreneurial philanthropy. Managerial Summary Entrepreneurs' philanthropic behavior depends not only on how high their perceived status is but also on how consistent it is across different domains. When entrepreneurs experience status inconsistency—for example, being economically successful but perceiving weaker social or political standing—this imbalance may be associated with either efforts to compensate through giving or feelings of resentment that suppress it. Overall, entrepreneurs with unbalanced perceived status tend to give less, yet those from lower‐class backgrounds are more inclined to donate in ways consistent with status‐balancing motives. Our study highlights how the interplay of economic, social, and political status perceptions relates to entrepreneurial philanthropy.
